Client 2: Yeah, and just… sanity-check that we’re not stretched too thin month to month.
Advisor: Perfect. Patrici, I’ll start broad on income—what do you two bring in gross per year, household total?
Client 1: Household gross is 226850 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing sources—James, you’re W-2 employed, and Patrici you’re self-employed, right?
Client 2: Yes, I’m employed. Patrici’s been self-employed forever—since the late 80s.

Advisor: Okay. Now on the spending side—about how much goes out each month for total living expenses, everything combined?
Client 2: It feels like around seventeen thousand a month.
Client 1: It is 17000 per month, yes—total monthly expenses.
Advisor: Thanks—one sec, I’m writing that down. Within that, what’s the monthly debt payment total—mainly the mortgage, I’m guessing?
Client 2: The mortgage is the big one. The monthly payment is 7050.

Client 1: It is 17000 per month, yes—total monthly expenses.
Advisor: Thanks—one sec, I’m writing that down. Within that, what’s the monthly debt payment total—mainly the mortgage, I’m guessing?
Client 2: The mortgage is the big one. The monthly payment is 7050.
Advisor: Okay, 7,050 monthly on the mortgage. And the rest of the 17,000 is the usual living costs—utilities, food, insurance, that kind of stuff.
Client 1: Exactly.

Client 1: Exactly.
Advisor: Great. Let’s shift to what you’ve built up. Roughly how much do you have in investable assets—retirement accounts, taxable investments, cash you’d invest—total?
Client 1: Total investable assets are 659850.
Advisor: Okay—thank you. And we’ll break that into the buckets in a minute so I understand what’s where.
Client 2: When you say “investable,” you mean not the house, right?

Client 1: Correct.
Advisor: Perfect—thanks. Let’s pin down the home and the mortgage next. Patrici, what do you use as the current value of the house?
Client 1: We use 381150 for the property value.
Advisor: Got it. And that’s your primary residence, just to be clear?
Client 1: Yes—primary residence.

Client 2: I think it’s like, around 260-ish? I don’t remember the exact number.
Advisor: No worries—ballpark is fine, but if you have the exact figure, that helps me model it cleanly.
Client 1: The mortgage balance outstanding is 260900.
Advisor: Great, thank you. And just confirming: when we say total loans outstanding for the household, that’s basically the same number because it’s only the mortgage—right?
Client 1: Correct. Loans outstanding total is 260900.

Client 2: I think it’s like, around 260-ish? I don’t remember the exact number.

Advisor: Hi Patrici, hi James—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we dive in, what would make this meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’d like an organized pass through income, expenses, what we’ve got saved, and the big obligations—then next steps.
Client 2: Yeah, and just… sanity-check that we’re not stretched too thin month to month.
Advisor: Perfect. Patrici, I’ll start broad on income—what do you two bring in gross per year, household total?
Client 1: Household gross is 226850 a year.

Advisor: Hi Patrici, hi James—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we dive in, what would make this meeting a win for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I’d like an organized pass through income, expenses, what we’ve got saved, and the big obligations—then next steps.
Client 2: Yeah, and just… sanity-check that we’re not stretched too thin month to month.
Advisor: Perfect. Patrici, I’ll start broad on income—what do you two bring in gross per year, household total?
Client 1: Household gross is 226850 a year.

Advisor: Great. And household size-wise, is it just the two of you as adults in the household—two adults total?
Client 2: Yep, 2 adults.
Advisor: Perfect. Next one is more about how we’ll work together. Are you engaging me through an RIA—registered investment advisor—relationship, or something else?
Client 1: It’s RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And then taxes: when you think about your marginal federal bracket, what band should I use right now?

Client 2: I always mix this up—are you asking like the percentage bracket?
Advisor: Yeah, exactly—the bracket percentage you’re mostly in, not your effective rate.
Client 1: Use 32%.
Advisor: Perfect. One sec—just noting that down.
Advisor: While I’ve got you, Patrici, I’m going to do a quick category sweep so nothing’s missing: income, assets, liabilities, and protection—sound okay?

Advisor: And James, you’ve also got a bank account balance that’s part of that investable total—cash reserves at the bank?
Client 2: Yes, we keep a chunk at the bank for liquidity.
Advisor: Okay. On liabilities, you mentioned the mortgage payment—do you have any other loans besides the mortgage right now?
Client 1: No, just the mortgage.
Advisor: Got it. And protection—Patrici, do you have life insurance in place that runs through 2035-03-02?

Client 1: Yes, that’s accurate.
Advisor: Okay. Household basics next—Patrici, James, just so I don’t assume anything: what’s your marital status?
Client 1: We’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And dependents—kids or anyone financially dependent on you right now?
Client 2: No—number of dependents is 0.

Client 1: Right.
Advisor: Risk comfort: earlier you both sounded pretty balanced—when you think about overall risk tolerance, what word fits best?
Client 1: moderate.
Advisor: Okay. And when you say moderate, Patrici, is that like “I can handle normal market swings but I don’t want anything that could blow up the plan”?
Client 1: Exactly—moderate, with guardrails.

Client 2: Yeah, and just… sanity-check that we’re not stretched too thin month to month.
Advisor: Perfect. Patrici, I’ll start broad on income—what do you two bring in gross per year, household total?
Client 1: Household gross is 226850 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing sources—James, you’re W-2 employed, and Patrici you’re self-employed, right?
Client 2: Yes, I’m employed. Patrici’s been self-employed forever—since the late 80s.

Advisor: Great—thank you. And James, your gross annual from employment is 91850, correct?
Client 2: Yes, 91850 gross annually.
Advisor: And Patrici, your gross annual is 135000?
Client 1: Correct—135000.
Advisor: Okay. Last category: protection. Patrici, you said you have life insurance through 2035-03-02—what’s the coverage amount and the monthly premium?

Advisor: Hi Patrici, hi James—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we dive in, what would make this meeting a win for you?

Client 2: Yeah, and just… sanity-check that we’re not stretched too thin month to month.
Advisor: Perfect. Patrici, I’ll start broad on income—what do you two bring in gross per year, household total?

Client 1: Yes, my gross annual income is 135000.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I describe what you do in plain English: what would you call your occupation group—like operations, sales, finance, that kind of bucket?
Client 1: Operations. That’s the closest fit.
Advisor: Okay, operations—makes sense. And employment status for you, Patrici—are we calling that self employed at this point?
